title: Quick-Pickled Vegetables

overview.description: Crisp vegetables preserved in a sharp, lightly sweet vinegar brine for a bright, crunchy accompaniment to rich and spicy dishes.

ingredients: 1 carrot, cut into thin matchsticks or ribbons
1 small red chilli, thinly sliced, optional
150ml white wine vinegar or rice vinegar
method: Prepare the vegetables and place them in a clean heatproof jar or container.
Put the vinegar, water, sugar and salt into a small saucepan.
Add the garlic and peppercorns if using.
Heat gently, stirring until the sugar and salt have dissolved.
Pour the warm pickling liquid over the vegetables.
Allow to cool to room temperature.
The vegetables can be eaten after approximately 30 minutes, but the flavour will improve after several hours.

learning.did-you-know: Quick pickling preserves vegetables through acidity rather than fermentation. The vinegar creates a sharp flavour while helping the vegetables keep for longer in the refrigerator.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How vinegar, water, salt and sugar can be balanced to create a quick pickle and how acidity can brighten rich or spicy dishes.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A simple and flexible way to add acidity, freshness and crunch to rich meals while introducing the children to the basic principles of pickling.
chapman-tips: Thinly sliced vegetables pickle much faster than thick pieces.
You don't need to pickle every vegetable in the same jar; experiment with different combinations.
Red onion is particularly useful because it becomes bright pink and softens quickly.
The pickle should taste sharp but balanced. Add a little more sugar if it is too aggressive.
A little chilli can add heat without making the pickle overwhelmingly spicy.
The vegetables should remain crisp rather than becoming completely soft.
Red onion + carrot → great with tacos and fajitas.
Cucumber + carrot → particularly good with Asian dishes.
Cabbage + carrot → good with gyoza and other richer foods.
Radish + red onion → excellent with roast chicken or tacos.
Jalapeño or other chilli → useful when you want a hotter pickle.
notes.batch-cooking: Make a batch and keep refrigerated for several days. The exact storage time depends on the vegetables used and how cleanly the pickle is prepared.
notes.leftovers: Use leftover pickled vegetables in sandwiches, wraps, burgers, tacos, rice bowls or alongside curries.
